name: Oliver Kahn
hints: Was the first goalkeeper to win the Golden Ball at a World Cup — in 2002 despite his team losing the final
Won the Champions League with a German club in 2001, saving three penalties in the shootout
Spent his entire peak career at Bayern Munich, winning 8 Bundesliga titles
Was known for his intimidating presence and nicknamed 'The Titan'
His howler in the 2002 World Cup final is remembered as one of the game's great goalkeeping errors

name: Didier Drogba
hints: Scored in his last-ever Champions League appearance — a 90th-minute equaliser in the final — before converting the winning penalty
Was the Chelsea top scorer in the Premier League four times
His goals helped end a 50-year wait for the Champions League at his club
Used his fame to help negotiate a ceasefire in an Ivory Coast civil war
Won the African Player of the Year award twice and is his nation's all-time top scorer

name: Samuel Eto'o
hints: Won the Champions League with two different clubs — Barcelona in 2006 and Inter Milan in 2010
Won back-to-back Champions Leagues with different clubs in consecutive seasons
Africa's all-time top scorer at the time of his peak and a four-time African Player of the Year
Was sold by Real Madrid as a teenager — they later tried and failed to re-sign him
Scored in both the 2006 and 2009 Champions League finals

name: Ruud van Nistelrooy
hints: Scored in 10 consecutive Champions League matches — a record that stood for years
Won the Premier League Golden Boot in 2002/03 with 25 goals
Suffered a serious knee injury on the day he was supposed to sign for his most famous club — and came back a year later
Was top scorer in the Champions League for three separate seasons
Fell out badly with the manager who signed him, leading to his departure from a Manchester club

name: Patrick Vieira
hints: Captained a north London side through the 2003/04 unbeaten season
Won the 1998 World Cup and Euro 2000 with France as a commanding defensive midfielder
His rivalry with Roy Keane is considered one of football's greatest individual battles
Won three Premier League titles at a north London club before leaving for Serie A
Was born in Senegal and grew up in France — representing France at international level

name: Ronaldo de Lima
hints: Considered by Pele and many others as the greatest footballer of all time
Won the World Cup in 1994 and 2002, scoring twice in the final of the latter
His career was devastated by three separate serious knee injuries, yet he kept returning
Had a peculiar haircut at the 2002 World Cup that became one of sport's most iconic images
Named FIFA World Player of the Year three times in a career that inspired a generation

name: Kaká
hints: Won the Ballon d'Or in 2007 — the last player not named Messi or Ronaldo to do so for over a decade
Won the Champions League with an Italian club in 2007 with a legendary individual performance against a German side
Was signed by Real Madrid for a then world record fee of £56m in 2009
A devout Christian who regularly points to the sky after scoring
Was nearly paralysed after a swimming pool accident as a teenager — which made him dedicate his life to football

name: Franck Ribéry
hints: Won the Champions League with a German club in 2013 as part of a treble-winning side
Finished third in the Ballon d'Or in 2013 — many felt he deserved to win it
Won 8 consecutive Bundesliga titles at a German club
His face bears distinctive scars from a serious car accident he survived as a toddler
Was part of the France squad that lost the 2006 World Cup final

name: Wayne Rooney
hints: England's all-time leading scorer — a record later surpassed by another Englishman
Scored an overhead kick against a rival Manchester club that is often cited as one of the Premier League's greatest goals
Made his top-flight debut at 16, scoring against the then-reigning European champions
Won 5 Premier League titles and a Champions League with a Manchester club
Was known as much for his combative temperament as for his remarkable talent and vision

name: Arjen Robben
hints: Won the Champions League in 2013, scoring the last-minute winner in the final
Won 8 consecutive Bundesliga titles at a German club
Was known for cutting inside from the right wing and scoring with his left foot — one of football's most predictable yet unstoppable moves
Missed a penalty in a World Cup final shootout before converting a winning penalty in the same tournament four years later
Was plagued by recurring hamstring injuries throughout his career despite consistently brilliant performances

name: Filippo Inzaghi
hints: Scored in two Champions League finals — both for the same Italian club
Was described by Sir Alex Ferguson as a player who was 'born in an offside position'
Won the Champions League in both 2003 and 2007 with the same club
Built his career entirely around poaching goals from close range and never miscontrolling
Was a serial winner in Serie A despite rarely being considered an elite-level player

name: Clarence Seedorf
hints: The only player in history to win the Champions League with three different clubs
Won the trophy with Ajax in 1995, Real Madrid in 1998, and AC Milan in 2003 and 2007
Was a technically complete midfielder — powerful, creative, and a goalscorer
Became a manager after retiring, taking charge of AC Milan and later Cameroon
Was born in Suriname and later became a Dutch citizen and international

name: Lev Yashin
hints: The only goalkeeper in history to win the Ballon d'Or — in 1963
Reportedly saved over 150 penalties in his career
Won 5 Soviet league titles and was an Olympic champion in 1956
Known entirely for wearing all-black — earning him the nickname 'The Black Spider'
The award given annually to the world's best goalkeeper is named in his honour

name: Johan Cruyff
hints: Won three consecutive European Cups with Ajax from 1971 to 1973
Invented a turn that became one of football's most famous individual skills — named after him
Won three Ballon d'Or awards and was regarded as the pioneer of Total Football
As a manager, his Barcelona team played a style that influenced Guardiola's tiki-taka era
Refused to play in the 1978 World Cup for personal safety reasons — his team reached the final without him

name: Franz Beckenbauer
hints: Won the World Cup as both a player in 1974 and as a manager in 1990 — only the second person to do so
Won the European Cup three consecutive times with Bayern Munich in the 1970s
Essentially invented the modern 'libero' or 'sweeper' position — a defender who carried the ball forward
Was nicknamed 'Der Kaiser' for his commanding elegance and leadership
Won the Ballon d'Or twice — in 1972 and 1976

name: Ronaldo Luis
hints: Won the World Cup in both 1994 and 2002, scoring a brace in the 2002 final
Named FIFA World Player of the Year three times despite multiple serious knee surgeries
Had a mysterious seizure-like episode the night before the 1998 World Cup final
His iconic haircut at the 2002 World Cup was reportedly copied by thousands of children worldwide
Often described as the greatest striker in football history by his contemporaries

name: Pep Guardiola
hints: Won La Liga and the Copa del Rey as a player at Barcelona but never won the Champions League as a player
Was the holding midfielder who orchestrated the Dream Team era of Barcelona under Johan Cruyff
Played in Italy, the Middle East, Mexico, and the USA at the end of his career
Had a defining role in Cruyff's greatest Barcelona side despite not being a goalscorer
Is far better known worldwide as a manager than as a player

name: Luis Figo
hints: Made one of the most controversial transfers in football history — from one rival to another
Won the Ballon d'Or in 2000 and the FIFA World Player of the Year the same year
Was pelted with a pig's head when he returned to the stadium of his former club
Won the Champions League with Real Madrid in 2002
Captained Portugal to the semi-finals of the 2006 World Cup at age 33

name: Michael Owen
hints: Won the Ballon d'Or in 2001 — the last Englishman to win the award
Scored one of the World Cup's most celebrated individual goals at age 18 against Argentina in 1998
Was plagued by hamstring injuries from his mid-20s onwards, limiting his peak years
Won the UEFA Cup, FA Cup, and League Cup in the same season at a Merseyside club
Became a controversial figure by joining a fierce local rival after leaving Newcastle

name: Raúl González
hints: Was Real Madrid's all-time top scorer before a Portuguese player surpassed him
Won three Champions League titles with Real Madrid between 1998 and 2002
Captained Real Madrid for many years and is considered one of the club's greatest ever players
Famously used to kiss his wedding ring after scoring — a tribute to his wife
Started at Atletico Madrid's youth academy before moving to rivals Real Madrid

name: George Best
hints: Won the European Cup in 1968 with Manchester United and was named the best player in Europe
Won the Ballon d'Or in 1968 — the first player from the British Isles to do so
Was nicknamed 'El Beatle' due to his celebrity status and long hair in 1960s Manchester
His off-field lifestyle became as legendary as his play — he was once described by himself in a famous self-deprecating quote
Widely considered one of the greatest dribblers in the history of the game

name: David Beckham
hints: Scored from inside his own half against Wimbledon in the 1996/97 Premier League opener
Won 6 Premier League titles and a Champions League with a Manchester club
Was the first British player to win league titles in four different countries
His free kick delivery was considered among the finest in the history of the game
Became one of the world's most recognised sports icons due to football and celebrity status

name: Dennis Bergkamp
hints: Refused to fly, which limited his availability in European away games but never diminished his impact
Scored a goal against Argentina in the 1998 World Cup quarter-final that is widely considered one of the greatest ever
Won three Premier League titles with Arsenal, including as part of the Invincibles
Was initially considered a disappointment at an Italian club before reinventing himself in England
A statue of him stands outside the stadium of the north London club he served for over a decade

name: Romario
hints: Won the 1994 World Cup with Brazil, scoring 5 goals and being named the tournament's best player
Scored over 1000 goals across his career by his own controversial counting
Was one of the most prolific scorers at PSV Eindhoven and FC Barcelona in the early 1990s
Fell out with his manager before the 1998 World Cup and was controversially dropped
Became a Brazilian politician after retiring from football

name: Eusébio
hints: Won the Ballon d'Or in 1965 after leading his club to the European Cup
Top scorer at the 1966 World Cup with 9 goals — including 4 in a single quarter-final
Spent almost his entire career at a single Portuguese club, winning 11 league titles
Born in Mozambique, he became Portugal's greatest-ever footballer of the 20th century
His statue stands outside the national stadium in Lisbon as a tribute to his legacy
